title: Coppice hazel dibber
subtitle: A pointed garden dibber from a coppice hazel rod, shaped with a sloyd knife
excerpt: A dibber is a pointed rod used to make planting holes in garden soil. A straight hazel rod from a coppice stool, thinned at one end to a blunt point and left with the bark on the grip section, is the simplest and most traditional form. One short session with a sloyd knife. A good beginner project for anyone who has never used a carving knife.
sourceNotes: Garden dibber and planting-stick forms from coppice rods documented in Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) and in British rural horticultural practice.

content.text: A garden dibber is a tool that asks nothing complex of the maker. A straight
coppice
hazel rod of one to two years' growth is already the right length and diameter. The work is to thin one end to a blunt working point with
push cuts
from a sloyd knife, and to leave the rest as it comes from the tree.
Green
hazel cuts easily and the bark on the grip section stays smooth and comfortable in the hand. The whole project takes less than half an hour.
What you need: wood

A straight green hazel rod, 30 to 40 cm long and 2 to 3 cm in diameter. A coppice-cut rod from one to two years' growth from the stool is the right size. The rod should be straight for at least the bottom 20 cm that will form the working end. Minor curves in the upper grip section are acceptable. Ash and willow rods of the same dimensions are equally suitable.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife and a leather strop. Nothing else. This is an intentionally minimal-tool project.
The work
Step 1: Mark the working section

Mark a line around the rod 20 cm from the pointed end with a knife scratch or pencil. Below this line is the working section that will be tapered; above it is the grip section that stays at full diameter with its bark intact.
Step 2: Remove the bark from the working section

Hold the rod in the four-finger grip, thumb on top. Use push cuts at a shallow angle to peel the bark from the working section. Green hazel bark peels in long strips with little force. Remove all bark from the lower 20 cm of the rod, stopping at the line. The peeled surface will be pale green-white.
Step 3: Taper the point

Starting at the mark, take push cuts along the working section angling toward the tip. The taper should begin at the line and run to the tip over 20 cm; the tip itself should be blunted to a blunt cone, not knife-sharp. A pointed dibber tip that is too sharp can core the soil rather than parting it to the sides; a blunt tip makes a clean rounded hole.

Work around the circumference of the rod rather than from one side only, so the taper is even. The finished working section should be about 12 mm in diameter 5 cm above the tip, tapering to 15 to 18 mm at the mark.
Step 4: Round the grip end
The top of the grip end is where pressure is applied when the dibber is pushed into soil. Round it to a dome with a few push cuts around the top end so it does not dig into the hand during use.
Finishing

No finish is needed or desired. The dibber is a garden tool that will be driven into soil repeatedly; any oil finish would wash off quickly. The green hazel wood will harden over several weeks of use and seasonal drying. The bark on the grip section will remain smooth and comfortable.
Variations
Drill through the grip section and slot a shorter rod through it. The cross-piece makes a T-bar dibber. You push down with your wrist instead of your palm.
Mark centimetre intervals on the working section with a permanent marker or by burning a series of rings with a pyrography tool. Use the marks as a depth gauge when planting bulbs.
Make a set of three in different diameters (1.5 cm, 2 cm, 2.5 cm diameter at the mark) for different seed and plug sizes.
Care

Store under cover; prolonged weathering will rot the grip bark eventually. A dibber made from hazel will last several seasons of regular use. When the point becomes rounded from use, re-taper it with a few push cuts.

items.fix: Score a deeper ring cut around the rod at the mark before peeling: press the knife perpendicular to the rod surface and work all the way round. The bark will then peel to a clean edge at the line.
items.cause: The boundary cut around the circumference was not deep enough to sever the bark cleanly.
items.symptom: The bark does not peel cleanly at the section boundary

items.fix: Sight along the working section from the tip looking up. Identify the thick side and take corrective passes on that face until the section is even.
items.cause: Push cuts were made predominantly from one side.
items.symptom: The taper is uneven; one side is thicker than the other

items.fix: Shorten the cut length and work around the rod in short passes rather than long ones. If a split has already started, it will not worsen if the cut direction is kept perpendicular to the split path.
items.cause: The grain in the rod is not straight; it runs at an angle. A push cut along the grain will sometimes follow the grain into the wood rather than across it.
items.symptom: The rod splits lengthwise during pointing
